title: From Pilot Trap to Institutional Capacity: A Governance Framework for Sustainable Clinical AI Implementation in Health Systems.
gap1_relaxed_trigger: N/A

interaction_evidence: This persistent gap reflects not only technical or regulatory limitations but also insufficient governance capacity within healthcare organizations

title: Prioritizing human-AI collaboration in healthcare: the TRIAD framework for trustworthy governance, real-world, and integrated adaptive deployment
gap1_evidence: TRIAD requires explicit data provenance and intended use, fairness auditing, and calibrated uncertainty. This framework evaluates the human-AI team in real workflows using team-level metrics
gap1_relaxed_trigger: proposes TRIAD evaluation framework with fairness auditing, team-level metrics, and subgroup impact monitoring addressing standard-practice limitations
gap2_evidence: Deployment proceeds via staged rollouts with preregistered guardrails and continuous monitoring of performance and subgroup impact.
gap2_indicators: explicit data provenance and intended use
gap2_relaxed_trigger: proposes concrete trustworthy governance pillar with preregistered guardrails, continuous monitoring, and explicit data provenance/intended use
gap3_evidence: Deployment proceeds via staged rollouts with preregistered guardrails and continuous monitoring of performance and subgroup impact.
gap3_relaxed_trigger: proposes integrated adaptive deployment framework with staged rollouts and preregistered guardrails (implementation framework for sustainable deployment)
interaction_evidence: TRIAD views intelligence as a property of the human-AI team rather than the AI model alone. Aligning governance, evaluation, and deployment around clinicians and patients enables durable gains
notes: Multi-gap framework covering all three dimensions as parallel pillars. Coded 0 on interaction because pillars are presented as aligned/parallel requirements, not as one gap causally producing another.

gap2_evidence: Regulators will need to advance flexible mechanisms to keep up with the pace of change in AI across biomedicine and health care.
gap2_indicators: coordination across industries and international organizations
gap2_relaxed_trigger: proposes flexible regulatory mechanisms with cross-industry and international coordination; postmarket monitoring as governance mechanism
notes: Authoritative FDA perspective; covers evaluation (lifecycle monitoring) and governance (flexible mechanisms) but does not specifically address implementation/scale-up frameworks.

title: Real-World Feasibility of Generative Large Language Models for Clinical Decision Support in Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia.

title: Real-World Demonstration of AI to Clinical Cytometry for Rapid and Reliable Decision Support and Automated Reporting of Blood Cancers Including B-NHL and Acute Leukemias

title: Ecological Validity Missing in AI-Assisted Clinical Decision Support Research: Why Real-World Context Matters
gap1_evidence: current experimental approaches often fail to capture the complexities of clinical environments in three critical dimensions: the temporal dynamics of decision-making, the holistic nature

proposes longitudinal evaluation, multi-stream integration, nuanced metrics

gap2_evidence: N/A
notes: Title itself is the gap indicator; paper is a direct critique of evaluation methodology with empirical case study support.

title: Governance and Accountability of AI Agents in U.S. Healthcare Systems: Ethical and Regulatory Perspectives
gap1_evidence: Outcomes were found to depend less on algorithmic precision and more on the maturity of lifecycle safeguards linking principles to auditable controls
gap1_indicators: future research: multi-site evaluations, drift registries
gap1_relaxed_trigger: documents empirical finding that outcomes depend on lifecycle safeguards (evaluation-relevant) and calls for multi-site evaluations and drift registries
gap2_evidence: governance bundles comprising clear intended use, human oversight, calibration and drift management, and operational telemetry were embedded, systems achieved higher diagnostic accuracy
gap2_relaxed_trigger: proposes concrete governance bundles with specified components (intended use, human oversight, calibration/drift management, operational telemetry)
gap3_evidence: weak workflow integration or absent monitoring diminished realized benefit
gap3_relaxed_trigger: documents that weak workflow integration and absent monitoring diminish realized benefit (empirical scale-up/adoption barrier)
interaction_evidence: Outcomes were found to depend less on algorithmic precision and more on the maturity of lifecycle safeguards linking principles to auditable controls
notes: Strong multi-gap systematic review with explicit causal language: governance maturity drives outcomes (performance + realized benefit). Lifecycle safeguards explicitly link governance to evaluation findings and to workflow integration outcomes.

title: Generative AI’s healthcare professional role creep: a cross-sectional evaluation of publicly accessible, customised health-related GPTs
gap2_evidence: None of the customised GPTs identified were FDA, EU MDR, or TGA-approved
gap2_relaxed_trigger: documents empirical regulatory gap (1055 health GPTs, none FDA/EU MDR/TGA approved) and questions whether regulations keep pace
notes: Empirical documentation of regulatory vacuum for consumer-facing health AI; clear governance gap.
